An investigation into the effect of time intervals on the
recall of words.

detailedExemplarData.title:
An investigation into the effect of time intervals on the
recall of words.

subject.title: Psychology
subject.description:
IB Psychology is a Group 3 subject in the International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP). The course examines human behavior and mental processes through scientific inquiry, exploring biological, cognitive, and sociocultural approaches to understanding behavior. Students develop critical thinking skills while investigating contemporary psychological research, research methods, and ethical considerations in psychological practice.

The course is available at Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours) and Higher Level (HL, 240 hours), with first assessment in 2027.

Assessment consists of Paper 1 (integration of concepts, content, and contexts through short-answer and extended-response questions: SL 2 hours, HL 2 hours, SL 50%, HL 40%), Paper 2 (application of psychological knowledge to research contexts: SL 1 hour, HL 2 hours, SL 25%, HL 20%), Paper 3 (HL only: research methods and data analysis, 1 hour, 20%), and Internal Assessment (research proposal development: SL 25%, HL 20%).

strengths: Clear statement and explanation of the investigation aim linked to multistore model and STM research
Well-described theory/model with explicit connection to the experimental manipulation
weaknesses: Hypotheses not fully operationalized (IV values and raw DV count not specified)
Connection between capacity limits and chosen rehearsal intervals not fully explained
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Two strands meet the top band descriptors (aim relevance; theory linkage) but the hypothesis strand sits in the mid band due to incomplete operationalization. Overall performance aligns with the 3â4 band, so 4/6 is appropriate.

strengths: Rationale provided for choosing independent-samples design to avoid order effects
Materials and controls described and justified to ensure consistency
weaknesses: Sampling rationale lacks full explanation of mixed random/self-selection methods
Omission of environmental (e.g. noise) controls weakens methodological rigor
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: All strands are described and partly explained, but notable gaps in sampling rationale and control of extraneous variables place the work solidly in the 1â2 band. A score of 2/4 reflects these omissions.

strengths: Appropriate choice of descriptive (mean, SD) and inferential (t-test) statistics
Graphs correctly formatted and directly address the hypothesis
weaknesses: Errors in statistical write-up (contradictory interpretation, missing t-value and degrees of freedom)
Findings interpretation is internally inconsistent and lacks clear linkage of p-value to conclusion
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Statistical and interpretative strands reach the mid band due to application of correct tests with errors, while graphical presentation is strong. Overall performance merits 4/6.

strengths: Strengths and limitations are well explained and linked to validity and generalizability
Modifications are explicitly justified in response to identified limitations
weaknesses: Discussion of findings is brief and does not deeply evaluate theoretical implications
Connections between theoretical predictions and unexpected results are underdeveloped
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Two strands meet the top descriptor but the findings discussion sits in the mid band. Aggregated performance corresponds to the 3â4 band, so 3/6 is assigned.

strandSelections.reasoning: Two graphs are provided: correctly formatted box-and-whisker plots and a bar chart with error bars and clear axis labels relating directly to the hypothesis.  No substantive graphical errors are evident, so the work merits the top band (score 5).

The student states the statistical findings (means, p-value) and attempts to relate them to the hypothesis, but the interpretation is internally inconsistent (concludes âno effectâ while claiming the null is rejected).  Because findings are linked to hypothesis yet partially mis-interpreted, the strand fits the 3â4 band, with a modest score.

Both null and research hypotheses are stated and the IV (presence vs absence of 20-s rehearsal) and DV (number of words recalled from a 20-word list) are identified.  However, the wording does not fully operationalise the IV (0 s vs 20 s is implied but not stated) and the DV is expressed as an âaverageâ rather than the *raw count* each participant will produce.  Hence the work fits the middle band rather than the top.

The student explains why a mixed random / self-selected sample of 16-18 year-old native English speakers was used (language comprehension, ecological validity, gender balance).  These reasons show understanding of how the sampling choice serves the investigation, fulfilling the âexplainedâ requirement.

The multistore memory model is outlined (sensory, STM, LTM, capacity limits, role of rehearsal) with supporting citations.  The connection to the investigation is then spelled out â the experiment manipulates rehearsal time to test whether information survives in STM / passes to LTM.  This meets the requirement for a full description *and* explanation of the link.

Results are discussed in relation to the multistore model and rehearsal literature, but the discussion is brief and does not deeply evaluate theoretical implications.  This satisfies âdescribed with referenceâ (3â4 band) rather than the fuller discussion required for 5â6.

The student clearly states the aim â to test the effect of a rehearsal interval on word recall â and then explicitly explains why this is relevant by linking it to rehearsal in the multistore model and to Peterson & Petersonâs classic STM research.  This explanatory link demonstrates the purpose and value of the investigation, satisfying the descriptor for the top band even though the explanation is concise rather than exhaustive.
Multiple strengths and limitations (design, sampling, materials) are not only listed but *explained* and explicitly tied to validity and generalisability, fulfilling the highest band descriptor.

Materials (smartboard, stopwatch, neutral 20-word list) and controls (standardised slideshow, equal timing) are all described and *reasons* for their use are provided (minimise bias, ensure consistency).  That explanatory element places the work in the top band for this strand.

Two concrete modifications (adding auditory presentation; using sentences/trigrams) are directly linked to identified limitations (single-modality stimulus, ecological validity) and fully justified.  This explicit linkage and justification meet the 5â6 band requirements.

The independent-samples design is not only described but justified (to avoid expectancy and order effects).  This goes beyond a bare description and constitutes an explanation, matching the upper band for this strand.

Descriptive (mean, SD, boxplots) and inferential statistics (independent-samples t-test) are applied and the choice of test is briefly justified.  However, the write-up contains errors: the direction of the result contradicts the p-value interpretation and the actual t statistic is absent.  Appropriate tests with mistakes correspond to the 3â4 band; a mid-band score is assigned.

annotations.comment: Controls such as standardized slides and timing devices are well described, but the description omits environmental controls (e.g., noise level). Consider describing how extraneous factors were minimized.

The introduction provides context on memoryâs importance, but it would benefit from an explicit statement of the investigationâs aim at the outset to orient the reader. Clarifying the aim early improves coherence and fulfills the introduction criterion.

The description of Atkinson and Shiffrinâs multistore model is clear and well cited, outlining sensory memory, STM, and LTM stages. This thorough theoretical background effectively supports the investigationâs conceptual framework.

The mention of Millerâs capacity limit is accurate, but the connection between capacity constraints and the chosen rehearsal intervals is not fully explained. Elaborate on how 7Â±2 items relates to the investigationâs design.

The null hypothesis is clearly stated, but it does not specify how the dependent variable will be measured (raw word count per participant). Explicitly operationalize the DV to meet the criterion for hypothesis specification.

The variables description identifies IV and DV but refers to an âaverageâ recall rather than individual counts. Operationalize the IV (0 s vs. 20 s) and DV (raw count) to align with the top band requirements.
The research hypothesis is concise and directional, clearly stating that participants with rehearsal time will recall more words. This meets the standard for a well-formulated research hypothesis.

The rationale for using an independentâsamples design is clearly explained, noting expectancy and orderâeffect avoidance. This justification demonstrates a deeper understanding of research design validity.

The sampling description lists 54 students aged 16â18 and gender balance, but the mix of random and selfâselected methods isnât fully explained. Clarify why each technique was chosen to strengthen the rationale.
Consider reporting an effect size (e.g., Cohenâs d) to quantify the magnitude of the difference between groups and strengthen the inferential analysis.

The discussion references the multistore model but does not deeply evaluate how the nonâsignificant result challenges or supports existing theory. Expand this link for a richer theoretical discussion.
The discussion of participant variability as a limitation is relevant but could be strengthened by noting how it specifically impacted recall performance relative to rehearsal time.
The dependent variable should be operationalized by specifying that each participant records the raw number of words recalled, rather than referencing STM capacity. This precision enhances clarity.
Table 1 correctly reports means and standard deviations, but presenting means with eight decimal places hinders readability. Round to two decimal places for clarity and conciseness.
The materials list including word list, stopwatch, and smartboard is clear and justified in terms of neutrality and presentation consistency. This satisfies the materials criterion.

The discussion of modern media context diverts from explaining choice of materials. Focus on why the specific word list was selected and how it minimizes bias rather than broader digital-media commentary.
The interpretation noting an âinsignificant differenceâ appears before inferential results are presented. Integrate p-value and t-test outcomes to substantiate claims of significance.
The sentence breaks off midâexplanation (â...smaller interquartile range thanâ). Ensure that all analytic descriptions are complete to avoid confusion and maintain coherence.
The boxâplot description cogently details quartiles and whiskers, directly addressing the hypothesis. This clear visualization supports accurate interpretation of variability.
The suggestion to use sentences or trigrams builds on ecological validity and directly addresses the singleâmodality limitation. This modification is well justified.
Consider including additional modifications, such as varying rehearsal intervals systematically to explore a doseâresponse effect. This could deepen the evaluation section.
Outlier thresholds are calculated but the text defers identification of outliers to an appendix. Summarize here that no outliers were found to make the analysis selfâcontained.
The bar graph caption is labeled as âTable 2â and lacks a figure label. Use a consistent figure caption and label it correctly to distinguish graphical elements from tables.

The t-test section misinterprets the p-value: p<0.05 supports rejecting the null, indicating an effect, yet the conclusion states no effect. Clarify this contradiction to accurately link findings to the hypothesis.
The writeâup omits the calculated t statistic and degrees of freedom. Include these values to fully report the inferential analysis.
The description of the independentâsamples design highlights its strength in avoiding order effects. This demonstrates awareness of design validity and enhances the evaluation section.

The conclusion concisely restates the outcome, but it omits reflection on the unexpected direction of significance and its theoretical implications. Incorporate these insights to enrich the wrapâup.
To deepen the conclusion, consider proposing specific future research questions, such as testing different stimuli types or participant populations, to guide subsequent investigations.
Link your first proposed modification (add auditory presentation) explicitly to the limitation of using only visual stimuli. This connection justifies the change more fully.
The conclusion provides a clear summary of the studyâs findings, effectively wrapping up the main result without overâinterpretation.
